title: Inheritance for Shared Behavior
summary: Create subclasses that reuse a base class so related catalog items share common setup and behavior without duplicated code.

sketchBlocks.title: Why use a base class for shared behavior?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When two classes have the same core data and methods, copying that code into both classes makes your project harder to maintain. **Inheritance** lets you put the shared behavior in one base class, then let subclasses reuse it.

Imagine a catalog app with books and movies. Both need a title and a way to show a summary. Instead of writing the same constructor twice, you can move the shared parts into `CatalogItem`.

```python
class CatalogItem:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

    def summary(self):
        return f"Item: {self.title}"

class Book(CatalogItem):
    pass
```

Step by step:

- `CatalogItem` is the base class.
- Its `__init__` method stores `title`.
- Its `summary()` method gives every item a shared behavior.
- `Book(CatalogItem)` means `Book` inherits from `CatalogItem`.
- `pass` means `Book` adds nothing yet, but it still gets the inherited constructor and method.

That means this works:

```python
book = Book("Deep Work")
print(book.summary())
```

Output:

```python
Item: Deep Work
```

The big idea is simple: put common behavior in one place, then let related subclasses reuse it.

sketchBlocks.title: How subclasses add their own data with super()
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A subclass often needs **all the shared setup from the base class** plus a few extra attributes of its own. That is where `super()` helps.

```python
class CatalogItem:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

class Book(CatalogItem):
    def __init__(self, title, author):
        super().__init__(title)
        self.author = author
```

Line by line:

- `Book` inherits from `CatalogItem`.
- `Book.__init__` receives both `title` and `author`.
- `super().__init__(title)` runs the parent class constructor.
- That parent constructor creates `self.title`.
- Then `self.author = author` adds the subclass-specific attribute.

Now the object has both pieces of data:

```python
book = Book("Deep Work", "Cal Newport")
print(book.title)
print(book.author)
```

Output:

```python
Deep Work
Cal Newport
```

Using `super()` avoids rewriting the parent setup by hand. If the shared setup changes later, you only update it in the base class.

sketchBlocks.title: Overriding a shared method
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Sometimes subclasses share a method name, but each subclass needs a different version of that behavior. That is called **overriding**.

```python
class CatalogItem:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

    def label(self):
        return self.title

class Book(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Book: {self.title}"

class Movie(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Movie: {self.title}"
```

What changed?

- The base class defines a general `label()` method.
- `Book` replaces that method with a book-specific version.
- `Movie` replaces it with a movie-specific version.
- All three classes still use the same method name: `label`.

Example:

```python
book = Book("Dune")
movie = Movie("Arrival")
print(book.label())
print(movie.label())
```

Output:

```python
Book: Dune
Movie: Arrival
```

This is useful because other parts of your program can call `label()` on different item types and still get the right result for each subclass.

sketchBlocks.title: Shared method names enable polymorphism
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When different subclasses all respond to the same method name, a service can work with a **mixed list of objects**. This is the practical value of inheritance and overriding.

```python
class CatalogItem:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

class Book(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Book: {self.title}"

class Movie(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Movie: {self.title}"

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Arrival")]

for item in items:
    print(item.label())
```

Step by step:

- `items` contains different subclasses.
- The loop does not need separate code for books and movies.
- It just calls `item.label()` each time.
- Python uses the correct subclass method for each object.

Output:

```python
Book: Dune
Movie: Arrival
```

This is **polymorphism** in action: one loop, one method call, different subclass behavior. In real projects, this keeps service code shorter and easier to extend.

quizDraft.title: Build a shared base class for catalog items
quizDraft.prompt: You are organizing a small library catalog. In `models/base_item.py`, complete the `CatalogItem` class so its constructor stores `title` and its `summary()` method returns `Item: <title>`. In `main.py`, create one `CatalogItem` with the title `Deep Work` and print its summary. This practice matters because it shows how one base class can hold behavior that later subclasses will reuse.
quizDraft.hint: Put the shared title setup in the base class, then return a formatted string from `summary()`.

help.concept: A base class is the place for behavior that multiple related subclasses should share. Here, the shared behavior is storing a title and producing one standard summary string.
help.hint_1: The constructor should assign the incoming title to `self.title`.
help.hint_2: `summary()` should build a string that starts with `Item: ` and then uses the stored title.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.base_item import CatalogItem

item = CatalogItem("Deep Work")
print(item.summary())
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.base_item import CatalogItem

item = CatalogItem("Deep Work")
print(item.summary())

semanticChecks.message: Define a CatalogItem class in models/base_item.py.
CatalogItem should accept a title when created.
Store the title on each CatalogItem instance.
summary() should return the formatted item text.

quizDraft.title: Use super() to reuse shared setup
quizDraft.prompt: You are adding books to the catalog app. `CatalogItem` is already provided in `models/base_item.py`. In `models/book.py`, complete the `Book` subclass so it inherits from `CatalogItem`, calls `super().__init__(title)`, and stores `author`. In `main.py`, create `Book("Deep Work", "Cal Newport")` and print the title on one line and the author on the next line. This practice matters because subclasses often need shared parent setup plus their own extra data.
quizDraft.hint: Let the parent class handle `title`, then add `author` in the subclass.

help.concept: `super()` lets a subclass reuse the parent constructor instead of copying the same attribute setup. The subclass can then add only the new attributes it needs.
help.hint_1: Inside `Book.__init__`, call the parent constructor with the title before setting `self.author`.
help.hint_2: The subclass header should name the parent class, and the object should end up with both `title` and `author` attributes.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Deep Work", "Cal Newport")
print(book.title)
print(book.author)
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Deep Work", "Cal Newport")
print(book.title)
print(book.author)

semanticChecks.message: Define a Book class in models/book.py.
Book should accept both title and author.
A Book instance should have inherited title data and its own author data.

quizDraft.title: Override label() in two subclasses
quizDraft.prompt: You are preparing labels for a media shelf. The base class `CatalogItem` is provided. In `models/book.py`, make `Book.label()` return `Book: <title>`. In `models/movie.py`, make `Movie.label()` return `Movie: <title>`. In `main.py`, create `Book("Dune")` and `Movie("Arrival")`, then print each label on its own line. This practice matters because overriding keeps one shared method name while allowing subclass-specific output.
quizDraft.hint: Both subclasses should keep the same method name, but each one returns a different prefix.

help.concept: Overriding means a subclass defines its own version of a method that already exists in the base class. The shared method name stays the same, but the behavior changes by subclass.
help.hint_1: Write a `label()` method in each subclass that uses `self.title`.
help.hint_2: One return string should start with `Book: ` and the other should start with `Movie: `.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

book = Book("Dune")
movie = Movie("Arrival")

print(book.label())
print(movie.label())
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

book = Book("Dune")
movie = Movie("Arrival")

print(book.label())
print(movie.label())

semanticChecks.message: Define a Book class in models/book.py.
Define a Movie class in models/movie.py.
Book.label() should return the book-specific label.
Movie.label() should return the movie-specific label.

quizDraft.title: Use one service with mixed subclasses
quizDraft.prompt: You are building a catalog report service. `Book` and `Movie` classes are provided and both define `label()`. In `services/report_service.py`, complete `build_report(items)` so it returns a list of labels by looping through the mixed item list and calling `label()` on each object. In `main.py`, print each returned label on its own line for `Book("Dune")` and `Movie("Arrival")`. This practice matters because polymorphism lets one service work with different subclasses through a shared method name.
quizDraft.hint: Loop through the items and call the same method on each object.

help.concept: Polymorphism means code can treat different subclasses in a uniform way when they share the same method name and base-class contract. The service does not need separate logic for each subclass.
help.hint_1: Create an empty list, append `item.label()` for each object, and return the list.
help.hint_2: The function should work for a mixed list because both subclasses respond to `label()`.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.report_service import build_report

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Arrival")]

for line in build_report(items):
    print(line)
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.report_service import build_report

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Arrival")]

for line in build_report(items):
    print(line)

semanticChecks.message: build_report(items) should return labels for a mixed list in order.

quizDraft.title: Share a discount method through inheritance
quizDraft.prompt: You are updating prices in a shop catalog. In `models/base_item.py`, complete `CatalogItem` so it stores `title` and `price`, and add `discounted_price(percent)` that returns the reduced price. In `models/book.py`, make `Book` inherit from `CatalogItem` and add `author`. In `main.py`, create `Book("Clean Code", 30, "Robert C. Martin")` and print the result of `discounted_price(20)`. This matters because shared business rules belong in the base class instead of being copied into every subclass.
quizDraft.hint: Put the discount calculation in the parent class so the subclass can reuse it unchanged.

help.concept: Inheritance is useful when subclasses need the same business rule. A discount formula belongs in the base class if books, movies, and other items should all calculate it the same way.
help.hint_1: The discounted price is the original price minus the given percent of that price.
help.hint_2: After `Book` inherits from `CatalogItem`, a `Book` object should be able to call `discounted_price()` without redefining it.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Clean Code", 30, "Robert C. Martin")
print(book.discounted_price(20))
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Clean Code", 30, "Robert C. Martin")
print(book.discounted_price(20))

semanticChecks.message: A Book should inherit discounted_price() from CatalogItem and calculate the reduced price correctly.

quizDraft.title: Count mixed catalog items with one shared method
quizDraft.prompt: You are building a quick inventory summary. `Book` and `Movie` should both inherit from `CatalogItem`, and `CatalogItem` should define `is_available()` that returns `True` when `stock > 0`. In `services/inventory_service.py`, complete `count_available(items)` so it returns how many objects in the mixed list are available. In `main.py`, use `Book("Dune", 3)` and `Movie("Arrival", 0)` and print the count. This matters because one shared base method lets a service work across different subclasses.
quizDraft.hint: Give both subclasses the same inherited availability rule, then count how many return `True`.

help.concept: A shared base-class method creates a common contract for subclasses. Services can then loop through mixed objects and call the same method on each one.
help.hint_1: `is_available()` should check the stored `stock` value in the base class.
help.hint_2: In the service function, increase the count only when `item.is_available()` is true.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.inventory_service import count_available

items = [Book("Dune", 3), Movie("Arrival", 0)]
print(count_available(items))
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.inventory_service import count_available

items = [Book("Dune", 3), Movie("Arrival", 0)]
print(count_available(items))

semanticChecks.message: Book should inherit is_available() and return True when stock is above zero.
Movie should inherit is_available() and return False when stock is zero.
count_available(items) should count available objects in a mixed list.

quizDraft.title: Choose the main benefit of inheritance here
quizDraft.prompt: In a catalog app, both `Book` and `Movie` need the same `title` setup and the same `summary()` method. Why is a base class the best fit?
quizDraft.hint: Look for the choice that reduces repeated code across related classes.

help.concept: Inheritance is most useful when related classes share the same core data or behavior and should reuse one implementation instead of duplicating it.
help.hint_1: Think about what happens if you copy the same constructor and method into several classes.
help.hint_2: The strongest answer is about centralizing shared behavior so changes happen in one place.

options: It lets unrelated classes share random variable names.
It stores shared setup and behavior once so subclasses can reuse it.
It forces every subclass to print the exact same output forever.
It removes the need to create objects from subclasses.

quizDraft.title: Identify what super() does in a subclass constructor
quizDraft.prompt: A `Book` subclass has `__init__(self, title, author)` and calls `super().__init__(title)`. What is that call doing?
quizDraft.hint: It reuses the parent class constructor work for the shared attribute.

help.concept: `super()` gives a subclass access to parent behavior, especially useful in constructors when the parent already handles shared attributes.
help.hint_1: Focus on which class is responsible for setting `title`.
help.hint_2: The call runs the parent constructor so the subclass does not have to rewrite the shared setup.

options: It creates a second Book object automatically.
It deletes subclass attributes before setup starts.
It runs the parent constructor to initialize inherited data.
It converts the subclass into a base class.

quizDraft.title: Pick the statements that show polymorphism
quizDraft.prompt: Which statements correctly describe polymorphism in a catalog service? Choose all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Look for ideas about one loop or service calling the same method on different subclasses.

help.concept: Polymorphism lets code work with different subclasses through a shared method name or base-class contract, so the caller does not need separate logic for each type.
help.hint_1: A mixed list is a clue, but only if the objects share a method the service can call.
help.hint_2: Correct choices describe one service using the same method name across different subclasses, even when each subclass returns different results.

options: A report service loops through `Book` and `Movie` objects and calls `label()` on each.
Each subclass can return a different result from `label()` while the service code stays the same.
Polymorphism means every subclass must have identical method bodies.
A mixed list of subclasses can be processed through one shared method name.

quizDraft.title: Order the steps in a subclass constructor
quizDraft.prompt: Put these steps in the usual order for a subclass constructor that reuses parent setup and then adds its own attribute.
quizDraft.hint: Start with the subclass definition, then reuse the parent setup before adding subclass-specific data.

help.concept: A subclass constructor usually receives all needed values, calls the parent constructor for shared attributes, and then sets its own extra attributes.
help.hint_1: The parent setup should happen before the subclass stores its extra field.
help.hint_2: Think of the flow as: define subclass constructor, run shared setup, then add subclass-only state.

quizDraft.title: Complete the subclass header
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the missing parent class name.
quizDraft.hint: The name in parentheses is the class being inherited from.

help.concept: A subclass names its parent class in parentheses so Python knows which class to inherit from.
help.hint_1: `Book` should inherit from the shared base class used for catalog items.
help.hint_2: Choose the class name that would hold common item behavior like `title` and shared methods.

quizDraft.title: Complete the overridden method call
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the method name that a report service should call on both subclasses.
quizDraft.hint: Use the shared method name from the label examples.

help.concept: Polymorphism depends on different subclasses responding to the same method name, so the service can call one method consistently.
help.hint_1: The method returns text like `Book: Dune` or `Movie: Arrival`.
help.hint_2: Choose the method name used for subclass-specific display text in the examples.
